sections.section_title: 36 Proof. For 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(A), we have: 𝑑 𝑑𝑡  𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷𝑎𝑒−𝑖𝑡𝐷 = 𝑖𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷[𝐷, 𝑎]𝑒−𝑖𝑡𝐷. It follows that: ∥[𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷, 𝑎]∥= ∥𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷𝑎𝑒−𝑖𝑡𝐷−𝑎∥=

paragraphs.text: ≤|𝑡|∥[𝐷, 𝑎]∥= |𝑡|𝐿(𝑎). Since A is dense in 𝔄for the C∗-norm, it follows that 𝐿∗(𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷) ≤|𝑡|. □ It follows that: Proposition 5.7. In the above setting, for any 𝜑: R →C a bounded Borel function whose Fourier transform ˆ𝜑is compactly supported, the operator 𝜑(𝐷) has finite Lipschitz commutant seminorm with respect to (𝔄, 𝐿), and more precisely: 𝐿∗(𝜑(𝐷)) ≤ ∫ R |𝑡|| ˆ𝜑(𝑡)|𝑑𝑡 Proof. Indeed by the Fourier inversion formula: 𝜑(𝐷) = ∫ R ˆ𝜑(𝑡)𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷𝑑𝑡 where the integral converges in the strong operator topology. Thus for 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(A): [𝜑(𝐷), 𝑎] = ∫ R ˆ𝜑(𝑡)[𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷, 𝑎]𝑑𝑡 where the integral converges in the strong operator topology, and it follows that: ∥[𝜑(𝐷), 𝑎]∥≤ ∫ R | ˆ𝜑(𝑡)|∥[𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷, 𝑎]∥𝑑𝑡≤ ∫ R |𝑡|| ˆ𝜑(𝑡)|𝑑𝑡  𝐿(𝑎). which shows that: 𝐿∗(𝜑(𝐷)) ≤ ∫ R |𝑡|| ˆ𝜑(𝑡)|𝑑𝑡. □ Now since 𝐶0(R) is generated by 1 √ 1+𝑥2 we see that by local compactness of 𝐷, 𝜑(𝐷) ∈B(H) is locally compact with respect to 𝔄for any 𝜑∈𝐶0(R): 𝑎𝜑(𝐷) and 𝜑(𝐷)𝑎are compact for all 𝑎∈𝔄. Furthermore since the set of functions in 𝐶0(R) whose Fourier transform is compactly supported is dense in 𝐶0(R), it follows that 𝜑(𝐷) ∈𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) for any 𝜑∈𝐶0(R). Corollary 5.1. In the above setting, for any 𝜑∈𝐶0(R), we have: 𝜑(𝐷) ∈𝐶∗ comm(𝔄).

37 6. Higher index theory In this section, we fix a proper locally compact quantum metric space (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) represented non-degenerately and amply on a Hilbert space H. Using our definition of Roe algebras, we deduce some aspects of Higher index theory for locally compact quantum metric spaces. As operators of finite spectral propagation with respect to the full algebra are difficult to construct in general we shall consider the following two types of Higher index: • The first version is valued in the relative commutant Roe algebras 𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) defined in section 3, for which one can use the functoriality properties. (Note that the larger 𝐶∗ 𝑞(𝔄) in the commutative case has been used to define the Higher index of some pseudodifferential operators). While this Higher index can be defined in general for Dirac operators coming from spectral triples, in order to define it for general elements of the K-homology of 𝔄, we shall need to assume the existence of a partition of unity that commutes sufficiently well with elements of 𝔄. • The second version is valued in the spectral Roe algebra with respect to an arbitrary commutative subalgebra 𝔐⊂𝔅⊂𝔄, (for instance 𝔅= 𝔐) for which one can construct finite propagation operators by the usual cutting procedure. We first introduce the dual algebras 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄) (respectively 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)) and show that under some assumptions on the regularity of commutants in 𝔄with respect to the Lipschitz norm, the K-homology of 𝔄can be obtained from the K-theory of 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)/𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) (respectively 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)), using the 6-term exact sequence of K-theory thus allows us to define a Higher index Ind : 𝐾𝑖(𝔄) →𝐾𝑖 �𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) (respectively 𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)). 6.1. Relative commutant Roe algebras valued index. Definition 6.1. Denote by 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄) the 𝐶∗-algebra generated by operators on H that commute with 𝔄up to compacts and are of finite relative commutant seminorm. 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄) = {𝑇∈B(H) : 𝐿∗(𝑇) < ∞and ∀𝑎∈𝔄: [𝑇, 𝑎] ∈K(H)} We further recall the following 𝐶∗-algebras used in Paschke duality: • C(𝔄) = {𝑇∈B(H) : ∀𝑎∈𝔄: 𝑇𝑎∈K(H), 𝑎𝑇∈K(H)} the set of operators that are locally compact. • D(𝔄) = {𝑇∈B(H) : ∀𝑎∈𝔄: [𝑇, 𝑎] ∈K(H)} the set of operators that commute up to compacts with 𝔄. Then it is known that: Theorem 6.2 (Paschke duality). 𝐾𝑖+1 (D(𝔄)/C(𝔄)) = 𝐾𝑖(𝔄).

38 Definition 6.3. We shall say that (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) has an approximate unit satisfying the commutant- Lipschitz condition if there exists a sequence of strictly positive constants 𝐶𝑛such that 𝐶𝑛→ 𝑛→∞0 and an approximate unit 𝑒𝑛made of local elements in 𝔐such that: ∥[𝑒𝑛, 𝑎]∥≤𝐶𝑛𝐿(𝑎) for all 𝑎∈𝔄 and for any compact set 𝐾in 𝜎(𝔐) there exists 𝑁such that 𝑒𝑁𝜒𝐾= 𝜒𝐾. Note that the above definition entails that for any 𝑛, there exists 𝐾𝑛⊂𝐿𝑛compact sets in 𝜎(𝔐) such that 𝑒𝑛𝜒𝐾𝑛= 𝜒𝐾𝑛and 𝑒𝑛𝜒𝐿∁ 𝑛= 0. Furthermore the above holds if 𝔄satisfies the inequality ∥[𝑎, 𝑏]∥≤𝐶𝐿(𝑎)𝐿(𝑏) for some 𝐶and there exists an approximate unit 𝑒𝑛made of local elements in 𝔐such that 𝐿(𝑒𝑛) →0 and, for any compact set 𝐾in 𝜎(𝔐), 𝑒𝑁𝜒𝐾= 1 for some 𝑁. Let us first record the following consequence of the characterization of locally compact quantum metric spaces and of the existence of commutant-Lipschitz approximate units: Lemma 6.4. If (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) a locally compact quantum metric space, 𝜇∈S(𝔄|𝔐) and (𝑒𝑛)𝑛an approximate unit of 𝔄in 𝔐satisfying the commutant-Lipschitz condition, then for all 𝑛: {𝑒𝑛𝑎| 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0} is totally bounded for the norm topology of 𝔄. Proof. Let 𝜀> 0 we shall show that {𝑒𝑛𝑎| 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0} can be covered by a finite number of balls of radius less than or equal 𝜀. Let 𝐾be a compact set such that 𝑒𝑛𝜒𝐾∁= 0 (i.e. 𝑒𝑛is supported within 𝐾). Let then 𝑁such that 𝑒𝑁𝜒𝐾= 𝜒𝐾. (i.e. 𝑒𝑁= 1 on 𝐾), and such that ∥[𝑎, 𝑒𝑁]∥≤𝜀𝐿(𝑎). Such 𝐾and 𝑁exist by assumption. By the characterization of locally compact quantum metric spaces (theorem 2.9), the set {𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑒𝑁| 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0} is totally bounded for the norm topology of 𝔄. It follows that there exists a finite set 𝐹⊂𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) with 𝐿(𝑏) ≤1 and 𝜇(𝑏) = 0 for all 𝑏∈𝐹such that for all 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) with 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1 and 𝜇(𝑎) = 0, there exists 𝑏∈𝐹such that: ∥𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑒𝑁−𝑒𝑛𝑏𝑒𝑁∥≤𝜀. We then have, since 𝑒𝑛𝑒𝑁= 𝑒𝑛: ∥𝑒𝑛𝑎−𝑒𝑛𝑏∥≤∥𝑒𝑛[𝑒𝑁, 𝑎]∥+ ∥𝑒𝑛[𝑒𝑁, 𝑏]∥+ ∥𝑒𝑛𝑎𝑒𝑁−𝑒𝑛𝑏𝑒𝑁∥≤3𝜀. Which shows the desired total boundedness. □ If 𝔄has an approximate unit satisfying the commutant-Lipschitz condition then in the above equality we can replace D(𝔄) by 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄) and C(𝔄) by 𝐶∗ comm(𝔄):

39 Theorem 6.5. If (𝔄, 𝔐, 𝐿) has an approximate unit satisfying the commutant-Lipschitz condition then the following equality of 𝐶∗algebras holds: 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)/𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) = D(𝔄)/C(𝔄). Before diving in the proof we recall the following lemma from [14] that will be useful in the proof: Lemma 6.6. Let 𝐼be an ideal of a 𝐶∗-algebra 𝐿. For any separable 𝐶∗-algebra 𝐷⊂I𝐿there is a positive contraction 𝑥∈I𝐼such that: • [𝑥, 𝑑] ∈I0𝐼for all 𝑑∈𝐷, • (1 −𝑥)𝑑∈I0𝐼for all 𝑑∈𝐷∩I𝐼. Proof of theorem 6.5. It suffices to show that D(𝔄) ⊂𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)+C(𝔄). Let 𝑒𝑛be an approximate unit of 𝔄in 𝔐with ∥[𝑒𝑛, 𝑎]∥≤𝐶𝑛𝐿(𝑎) for all 𝑎∈𝔄. By eventually taking a subsequence of (𝑒𝑛)𝑛, we can assume 𝐶𝑛≤ 1 2𝑛+2. Let 𝑓𝑛= 𝑒𝑛−𝑒𝑛−1 with 𝑓1 = 𝑒1. We therefore have that: ∥[ 𝑓𝑛, 𝑎]∥≤𝐿(𝑎) 2𝑛 for all 𝑎∈𝔄, 𝑛∈N, and ∑︁ 𝑛 𝑓𝑛= 1 strictly. If 𝑇∈D(𝔄), then applying lemma 6.6 for the separable 𝐶∗-algebra 𝐷generated by 𝑇and 𝔄, viewed as constant elements of IB(H), and with 𝐼= K(H) viewed as an ideal of 𝐿= B(H). We get that there exists 𝑥∈IK(H) such that: • [𝑥, 𝑑] ∈I0K(H) for all 𝑑∈𝐷, • (1 −𝑥)𝑑∈I0K(H) for all 𝑑∈𝐷∩IK(H). taking 𝑦= 1 −𝑥, and denote by 𝑦𝑡the evaluation at 𝑡, we have in particular that: (1) [𝑦𝑡𝑇, 𝑎] → 𝑡→∞0 for all 𝑎∈𝔄: Indeed [𝑦𝑡𝑇, 𝑎] = (1 −𝑥𝑡)[𝑇, 𝑎] + [𝑥𝑡, 𝑎]𝑇, looking at the terms of the sum, [𝑇, 𝑎] is an element of 𝐷∩IK(H) thus (1 −𝑥𝑡)[𝑇, 𝑎] →0 and 𝑎is in 𝐷thus [𝑥𝑡, 𝑎]𝑇→0. (2) (1 −𝑦𝑡)𝑇is compact (thus locally compact): Indeed (1 −𝑦𝑡)𝑇= 𝑥𝑡𝑇compact since 𝑥∈IK(H). Now we are going to construct an operator ˜𝑇of the form ˜𝑇= ∑︁ 𝑛 𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛, for a well chosen sequence 𝑡𝑛→∞, such that: 𝐿∗( ˜𝑇) < ∞and 𝑇−˜𝑇∈C(𝔄). Fix 𝜇a local state on 𝔄. For any 𝑛∈N, by the lemma above the set { 𝑓𝑛𝑎: 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄), 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, 𝜇(𝑎) = 0}

40 is totally bounded since it is a sum of two totally bounded sets. Now, since [𝑦𝑡𝑇, 𝑓𝑛𝑎] → 𝑡0 for all 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) (property (1) above), we can choose 𝑡𝑛large enough such that for all 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) with 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1 and 𝜇(𝑎) = 0: ∥[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇, 𝑓𝑛𝑎]∥≤1 2𝑛. By eventually taking a larger 𝑡𝑛, we can furthermore require that: ∥[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇, 𝑓𝑛]∥≤1 2𝑛. Then we have: 𝑎𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛= 𝑓𝑛𝑎𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇+ [𝑎, 𝑓𝑛]𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇+ 𝑎[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇, 𝑓𝑛]. It follows that, for 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) with 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1 and 𝜇(𝑎) = 0: ∥[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛, 𝑎]∥= ∥𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛𝑎−𝑎𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛∥ ≤∥(𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛𝑎−𝑓𝑛𝑎𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇)∥+ ∥[𝑎, 𝑓𝑛]𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇∥+ ∥𝑎[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇, 𝑓𝑛]∥ ≤∥[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇, 𝑓𝑛𝑎]∥+ ∥[𝑎, 𝑓𝑛]𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇∥+ ∥𝑎[𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇, 𝑓𝑛]∥ ≤3 2𝑛 By summing over 𝑛it follows that [ ˜𝑇, 𝑎] ≤3 for all 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔲𝔄) with 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1 and 𝜇(𝑎) = 0. Now if 𝑎∈𝔰𝔞(𝔄) with 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1, then setting ˜𝑎= 𝑎−𝜇(𝑎)1, we have that 𝐿( ˜𝑎) = 𝐿(𝑎) ≤1 and 𝜇( ˜𝑎) = 0, thus: ∥[ ˜𝑇, 𝑎]∥= ∥[ ˜𝑇, ˜𝑎+ 𝜇(𝑎)1]∥= ∥[ ˜𝑇, ˜𝑎]∥≤3. It follows that: 𝐿∗( ˜𝑇) ≤3 < ∞. Finally, we have that: 𝑇−˜𝑇= ∑︁ 𝑛 𝑇𝑓𝑛−𝑦𝑡𝑛𝑇𝑓𝑛= ∑︁ 𝑛 (1 −𝑦𝑡𝑛)𝑇𝑓𝑛. If 𝑎is an element of 𝜒𝐾𝔄𝜒𝐾∩𝔄for a compact 𝐾in 𝔐, then by the assumption on the approximate unit 𝑒𝑛, there exists 𝑁such that for all 𝑛≥𝑁, 𝑓𝑛𝑎= 0. It follows that the sum Í 𝑛(1 −𝑦𝑡𝑛)𝑇𝑓𝑛𝑎is a finite sums of compact terms, (see property (2) above), from which we see 𝑇−˜𝑇∈C(𝔄). We conclude that D(𝔄) ⊂𝐷∗ comm(𝔄) + C(𝔄) and thus the two quotients are equal. □ Corollary 6.1. With 𝔄as in theorem 6.5, we have the following isomorphism: 𝐾𝑖+1 �𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)/𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) = 𝐾𝑖(𝔄) Now, if we consider the following short exact sequence, 0 →𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) →𝐷∗ comm(𝔄) →𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)/𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) →0 we get a boundary map: Ind : 𝐾𝑖+1(𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)/𝐶∗ comm(𝔄)) →𝐾𝑖(𝐶∗ comm(𝔄)).

41 In particular if 𝔄satisfies the conditions of theorem 6.5, we get a map: Ind : 𝐾𝑖(𝔄) →𝐾𝑖(𝐶∗ comm(𝔄)). Definition 6.7. We call the above map the Higher index map of 𝔄valued in the relative commutant Roe algebra of 𝔄. In particular if (𝔄, H, 𝐷) is a nonunital spectral triple (and without additional assumption on 𝔄 or on the eventual topography) we can construct an element of 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄), and thus a Higher index associated with dirac operator 𝐷as follows. Let us first recall the following denomniation: Definition 6.8. A bounded function 𝜓: R →R is called a normalizing function if it is odd, and satisfies lim𝑥→∞𝜓(𝑥) = 1 and lim𝑥→−∞𝜓(𝑥) = −1. Proposition 6.1. Let (𝔄, H, 𝐷) be a nonunital spectral triple, and let 𝜓be a normalizing function then 𝜓(𝐷) ∈𝐷∗ comm(𝔄), furthermore the class [𝜓(𝐷)] ∈𝐷∗ comm(𝔄)/𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) does not depend on the choice of 𝜓. It follows that we get an element Ind(𝐷) = Ind(𝜓(𝐷)) ∈𝐾𝑖(𝐶∗ comm(𝔄)). Proof. Recall first that if 𝜓1 and 𝜓2 are two normalizing functions then 𝜓1 −𝜓2 ∈𝐶0(R), thus 𝜓1(𝐷) −𝜓2(𝐷) ∈𝐶∗ comm(𝔄). The operator 𝜓(𝐷) commutes up to compacts with 𝔄: We reproduce for completeness what we need from the proof in [15] (section 3) and [16] (theorem 4.1). Consider the normalizing function 𝜓0 : 𝑥↦→ 𝑥 √ 1+𝑥2 then for 𝑎and 𝑏such that [𝐷, 𝑎] is bounded we have: [𝜓0(𝐷), 𝑎]𝑏= [𝐷(1 + 𝐷2)−1 2, 𝑎]𝑏= [𝐷, 𝑎](1 + 𝐷2)−1 2 𝑏+ 𝐷[(1 + 𝐷2)−1 2, 𝑎]𝑏 [𝐷, 𝑎](1 + 𝐷2)−1 2 𝑏is compact since it is the product of a bounded operator with a compact one. Furthermore using the integral formula for the functional calculus of (1 + 𝐷2)−1 2 we have: 𝐷[(1 + 𝐷2)−1 2, 𝑎]𝑏= 1 𝜋 ∫∞ 0 𝜆−1 2 𝐷2(𝜆+ 1 + 𝐷2)−1[𝐷, 𝑎](𝜆+ 1 + 𝐷2)−1𝑏𝑑𝜆+ 1 𝜋 ∫∞ 0 𝜆−1 2 𝐷(𝜆+ 1 + 𝐷2)−1[𝐷, 𝑎](𝜆+ 1 + 𝐷2)−1𝐷𝑏𝑑𝜆 Each of the two integrals above converges in operator norm and is compact since it is the norm limit of compact operators (using that (𝜆+ 1 + 𝐷2)−1𝑏is compact). Thus [𝜓0(𝐷), 𝑎]𝑏is compact, and by density of A in 𝔄, we see that it is compact for any 𝑎and 𝑏in 𝔄. It follows [𝜓0(𝐷), 𝑎𝑏] = [𝜓0(𝐷), 𝑎]𝑏+ 𝑎[𝜓0(𝐷), 𝑏] is compact as well. By density of products in 𝔄, it follows that [𝜓0(𝐷), 𝑎] is compact for any 𝑎∈𝔄. For a general normalizing function 𝜓, we can write 𝜓= 𝜓0 + 𝜙where 𝜙∈𝐶0(R) thus 𝜙(𝐷) is locally compact, it follows that [𝜓(𝐷), 𝑎] is compact for any 𝑎∈𝔄. The operator 𝜓(𝐷) is in 𝐷∗ comm(𝔄): Taking a normalizing function 𝜓1 whose Fourier transform is compactly supported (such a function exists), we have by proposition 5.7 that 𝐿∗(𝜓1(𝐷)) < ∞. It follows by the previous step that 𝜓1(𝐷) ∈𝐷∗ comm(𝔄).

42 Now, again for a general normalizing function 𝜓, we can write 𝜓= 𝜓1 + 𝜙where 𝜙∈𝐶0(R) thus 𝜙(𝐷) ∈𝐶∗ comm(𝔄) by corollary 5.1, it follows that 𝜓(𝐷) ∈𝐷∗ comm(𝔄). □ 6.2. Lipschitz controlled K-theory and quantitative K-theory, a comparison for finite Assouad- Nagata dimension. In this brief section, we note that the above construction of localized relative commutant Roe algebras, along with theorem 3.16, allows to compare two different approaches to approximate K-homology of metric spaces, namely quantitative K-theory [17] [18] and Lipschitz controlled K-theory [19]. With the filtration given by the propagation prop, quantitative K-theory of the Roe algebra was used to approximate K-homology. While Lipschitz controlled K-theory [19] was mainly used to approximate K-theory of metric spaces, but if we instead consider the Lipschitz controlled K-theory for the relative commutant algebras introduced above, with Lipschitz seminorm 𝐿∗, and in the case of metric spaces of finite Assouad-Nagata dimension, then this is equivalent to using quantitative K-theory to approximate the K-homology of the space as in [18] for instance. We invite the interested reader to refer to [19] and [17] [18] for the definitions of these two notions of controlled K-theory, the result then follows immediately from theorem 3.16. 6.3. Index valued in the Roe algebra of a commutative subalgebra. Let 𝔅be commutative subalgebra of 𝔄, such that 𝔐⊂𝔅⊂𝔄. Then we have defined in section 3 the propagation with respect to 𝔅and the corresponding Roe algebra 𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)  𝐶∗ spec(𝔅). We can then define the following dual algebra: Definition 6.9. Denote by 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) the 𝐶∗-algebra generated by operators on H that commute with 𝔄up to compacts and are of finite spectral propagation with respect to 𝔅. 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) = {𝑇∈B(H) : prop𝔅(𝑇) < ∞and ∀𝑎∈𝔄: [𝑇, 𝑎] ∈K(H)} Again we have the following: Theorem 6.10. The following equality of 𝐶∗algebras holds: 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) = D(𝔄)/C(𝔄). Proof. It suffices to show that D(𝔄) = 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) + C(𝔄). In other words, it suffices to show that any operator that commutes with 𝔄up to compact can be decomposed into a ”finite propagation” part and a locally compact part. Since 𝔄is proper, 𝜎(𝔅) is a proper separable metric space (in the classical sense), we can fix a partition of unity 𝜒𝑛∈𝔅such that diam (supp (𝜒𝑛)) ≤1. Now for 𝑇∈D(𝔄), let 𝑇′ = ∑︁ 𝑛 √𝜒𝑛𝑇√𝜒𝑛. Then • 𝑇′ is well-defined and prop(𝑇′) ≤1:

43 Indeed for 𝑎∈𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄|𝐾) where 𝐾is compact in 𝜎(𝔐), then the sum √𝜒𝑛𝑇√𝜒𝑛𝑎has a finite number of non-zero terms, and we can conclude strong convergence of the sum since 𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄|∗) is dense and the representation is non-degenerate. Furthermore if 𝑓, 𝑔∈𝔅have supports of distance at least 1, then 𝑓√𝜒𝑛𝑇√𝜒𝑛𝑔= 0, since 𝑓having support in supp(𝜒𝑛) would imply that 𝑔does not and vice versa. Thus 𝑓𝑇′𝑔= 0 and prop(𝑇′) ≤1. • 𝑇−𝑇′ is locally compact. Indeed (𝑇−𝑇′)𝑎= ∑︁ 𝑛 (𝑇𝜒𝑛−√𝜒𝑛𝑇√𝜒𝑛𝑎) = ∑︁ 𝑛 [𝑇, √𝜒𝑛]√𝜒𝑛𝑎 For 𝑎∈𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄|∗) this is a finite sum of compact operators and is thus a compact. We can conlude since 𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄|∗) is norm-dense and K(H) is closed. Note that in the above we get that furthermore 𝑇′ commmutes up to compacts with 𝔄, since 𝑇and 𝑇−𝑇′ both do. Thus 𝑇′ ∈𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) and 𝑇∈C(𝔄). Furthermore, it is clear each √𝜒𝑛𝑇√𝜒𝑛has finite spectral propagation with respect to 𝔅, thus so does 𝑇′. □ Remark 6.11. The only difference with the case where 𝔄is commutative is that we need to assume that 𝔏𝔬𝔠(𝔄|∗) is dense, this is true for proper quantum metric spaces. Corollary 6.2. 𝐾𝑖+1  𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)  = 𝐾𝑖(𝔄) Considering the following short exact sequence, 0 →𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) →𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) →𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) →0 We get boundary maps: Ind : 𝐾𝑖(𝔄) →𝐾𝑖(𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)) = 𝐾𝑖(𝐶∗(𝜎(𝔅))) . 6.3.1. K-homology through finite propagation valued localization algebras. Definition 6.12. Define 𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) to be the closure of the set of uniformly continuous bounded maps 𝑓: [0, ∞) →𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄), with bounded propagation with respect to 𝔅, such that for all 𝑎∈𝔄, [ 𝑓(𝑡), 𝑎] → 𝑡→∞0. We then have the following: Theorem 6.13. We have a natural isomorphism: 𝐾𝑖  𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄)   𝐾𝑖(𝔄) .

44 Under the above isomorphisms the coarse index is induced by the evaluation map ev0 : 𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) →𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)  𝐶∗(𝜎(𝔅)) given by ev0( 𝑓) = 𝑓(0). Ind = (ev0)∗: 𝐾𝑖  𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄)  →𝐾𝑖(𝐶∗(𝜎(𝔅))) We first introduce the corresponding “compactly commuting” algebras: Definition 6.14. Define 𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) to be the closure of the set of uniformly continuous bounded maps 𝑓: [0, ∞) →𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) such that for all 𝑎∈𝔄, [ 𝑓(𝑡), 𝑎] → 𝑡→∞0. Then we prove the following path regularization lemma: Lemma 6.15. 𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) = I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) Proof. It suffices to show that I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) = 𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) + I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄). If 𝑓is in I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) we construct ˜𝑓∈𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) such that 𝑓−˜𝑓is in I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄). For this, let 𝐵∗be the 𝐶∗-algebra generated by operators of finite propagation with respect to 𝔅, and let 𝐾∗= 𝐵∗∩K(H). The algebra 𝐾∗is an ideal of 𝐵∗. Furthermore, let 𝐷the separable 𝐶∗-algebra generated by { 𝑓} and by the elements of 𝔄viewed as constant functions. The elements of 𝔄are limits of finite propagation operators in 𝜒𝐾𝔄𝜒𝐾for 𝐾compact in 𝜎(𝔐), thus 𝔄⊂𝐿∗and thus 𝐷⊂I𝐵∗. Using lemma 6.6, we can find 𝑥∈I𝐾∗such that [𝑥, 𝑑] → 𝑡→∞0 for all 𝑑∈𝐷and (1 −𝑥)𝑑→ 𝑡→∞0 for all 𝑑∈𝐷∩I𝐾∗. Then setting ˜𝑓= 𝑓−𝑥𝑓: • 𝑓−˜𝑓= 𝑥𝑓is in I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄). Indeed 𝑥∈I𝐾∗, thus 𝑥𝑓is a uniformly continuous path of operators in 𝐾∗· 𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) ⊂𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄). • ˜𝑓∈𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄). That it is in I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) is obvious from the above, furthermore, for 𝑎∈𝔄, [ ˜𝑓(𝑡), 𝑎] = [𝑥, 𝑎] 𝑓(𝑡) + (1 −𝑥)[ 𝑓(𝑡), 𝑎] → 𝑡→∞0, by applying the conditions on 𝑥above to 𝑑= 𝑓and 𝑑= 𝑎respectively. □ Using the following short term exact sequence: 0 →𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) →𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) →I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) →0 We obtain boundary maps 𝐾𝑖+1  I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)  →𝐾𝑖  𝐶∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄)  . It suffices then to show that: • We have the vanishing formula: 𝐾𝑖  𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄)  = 0.

45 • The map induced by constant functions 𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) →I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄) induces an isomorpism on K-theory: 𝐾𝑖  I𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/I𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)  = 𝐾𝑖  𝐷∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)/𝐶∗ spec,𝔅(𝔄)  . The proof of these two points relies on an Eilenberg swindle argument with the following two observations: • Independence of the Roe algebra of 𝔅( recall 𝔅is commutative), and of the localization algebra on the representation used 𝜌. (Take an intertwining path of isometries for the localization algebras and cut it by elements in 𝔅to get finite propagation with respect to 𝔅). • The fact that if 𝑓∈𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) then ˆ𝑓∈IB(H ⊕∞) defined by: ˆ𝑓(𝑡) := ∞ Ê 𝑛=0 𝑓(𝑡+ 𝑛) is also in 𝐷∗ 𝐿,spec,𝔅(𝔄) with respect to the representation É∞ 𝑛=0 𝜌, since for fixed 𝑡, [ ˆ𝑓(𝑡), 𝑎] is a direct sum of compact operators of vanishing norm, it is compact. The rest of the proof then follows from the same arguments as in the classical case (see [20]). 7. Outlook In this paper we have established a link between locally compact quantum metric spaces and noncommutative coarse geometry, furthermore we have defined notions of Roe algebras and higher index theory in this framework. We believe that we have only scrached the surface of this new connection, and there are many open questions and directions that we leave for future research, in particular in connection with other developments in other fields of noncommutative geometry. We outline some of these directions below. 7.1. More examples of spectral Roe algebra computations. One direction is to compute the spectral Roe algebra for more examples of locally compact quantum metric spaces with more fundamental noncommutativity. One promising example is the Moyal plane, with the spectral triple strucuture defined on it in [21] and considered as locally compact quantum metric space as in [3]. We expect that in such cases, the relative commutant Roe algebra and the spectral Roe algebra will differ significantly. Indeed the relative commutant Roe algebra, along with the noncommutative coarse structure arising from it, looks to be more natural in the noncommutative setting. 7.2. Geometric realization of (finite) W*-quantum metric spaces. In relation with the point above, it would be interesting to study, even in the simplest cases (for instance M𝑛(C) with a Lipschitz seminorm), what kind of W* quantum metric structure arise from the spectral propagation, and from the spetral propagation with respect to the diagonal subalgebra C𝑛, furthermore one could study what happens when different representations of M𝑛(C) are considered. As arbitrary quantum graphs (and in particular those giving rise to quantum expanders) can be naturally viewed in the framework of quantum relations and thus of W* quantum metric spaces, this could allow us to

46 construct “geometric realizations” of quantum graphs (and in particular quantum expanders) in the sense of Lipschitz seminorms. 7.3. Spectral triples. In [4], W* quantum metric structures were associated to spectral triples, roughly by declaring that 𝑒𝑖𝑡𝐷has propagation |𝑡|. In our construction, we have shown that locally compact quantum metric spaces with a faithful representation give rise to W* quantum metric spaces. It would be interesting to compare these two constructions, and see if they agree in some cases. Again we expect this to fail in general and that the relative commutant Roe algebra is the closer concept. 7.4. Index theory. While we have defined two types of higher indices for locally compact quantum metric spaces, and in parallel with the classical case, it would be interesting to study more concrete examples and compute these indices in some cases. The Moyal plane appears to be a promising example in this direction as well. 7.5. 𝐿∗seminorms. The seminorm 𝐿∗satisfies basic properties that make it a good candidate for a noncommutative Lipschitz seminorm, aside from the fact that 𝐿∗(𝑇) = 0 does not imply that 𝑇is a scalar multiple of the identity. For finite dimensional algebras represented irreducibly on a finite dimensional Hilbert H𝐹space, this issue does not arise, and one gets a Lipschitz seminorm on B(H𝐹). It would be interesting to study this seminorm in more detail, and see if it can be used to construct new examples of (finite) quantum metric spaces, or if it can be used to give new insights on existing examples. For more general, nonfinite spaces, one can ask if one can take subspaces or quotient spaces of the relative commutant Roe algebra where 𝐿∗is a Lipschitz seminorm, and see if this can lead to new examples of quantum metric spaces.
